ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a combined hotplate beef and squid dish. FOODON_03315091 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent anchor for the beef component. The squid component is not separately anchored.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent for the beef component. No combined beef-squid hotplate code exists in FoodEx2. Squid component not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for a combined hotplate beef and squid set dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hotplate set serving of approximately 350 g, comprising sizzling beef slices and squid pieces with sauce, typically served with steamed rice in a Singaporean zi char context.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables and sauce (composite estimate) and Squid, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for hotplate beef and squid set exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked beef (NDB 13365, beef, short loin, cooked, broiled) and USDA entry for squid cooked (NDB 15150), combined in an approximate 60:40 beef-to-squid ratio by protein mass, with sauce contribution estimated from USDA soy-sauce and oyster-sauce entries. FAO/INFOODS SEA data for similar zi char mixed meat-seafood dishes were used to cross-check sodium and energy values. The composite per-100g values represent the edible portion of the mixed dish excluding rice.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed meat-sauce dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hotplate beef and squid set. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy with mixed meat dishes with savory sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., beef stir-fry with sauce, GI ~50–55). The carbohydrate content is low (5.4 g per 100g), primarily from sauce ingredients (oyster sauce, soy sauce). GL per serving calculated as: (52 × 18.9 g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 9.8, rounded to 10.2 accounting for sauce sugars. This is a low-GI, low-GL dish when consumed without rice.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100g; ~1820 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 350g serving provides approximately 1820 mg sodium, representing over 75% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low-to-moderate glycemic index (~52) with low glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low carbohydrate content (~5.4 g per 100g) and an estimated GI of 52, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added rice or high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef (~2.1 g per 100g) and high sodium from sauce base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contribution from beef and the high sodium load from the sauce; choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting reduced sauce can improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotplate Beef and Squid Set (铁板牛肉苏东套餐) is a popular Singaporean zi char dish featuring tender slices of beef and squid (sotong) cooked together on a sizzling cast-iron hotplate. The dish is seasoned with a savory sauce base typically comprising soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, producing a rich umami flavor with a slightly caramelized aroma from the high-heat hotplate cooking. The beef is typically thinly sliced and marinated before cooking, yielding a tender texture, while the squid is cut into rings or pieces and cooked briefly to maintain a slightly chewy, succulent bite. The dish is commonly served as part of a set meal at zi char stalls and casual restaurants across Singapore, often accompanied by steamed white rice. Despite its Chinese-style preparation, it appears on menus at establishments also offering Korean and Japanese cuisine, reflecting Singapore's multicultural food landscape. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality protein (~16.8 g per 100g) and provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, zinc, iron, and selenium from both the beef and squid components. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100g) derived from the sauce base, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without large portions of rice. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish/mollusc allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for hotplate beef and squid set exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy cooked beef and cooked squid entries with sauce contribution, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA data for similar zi char dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for these components. 2. The input record labels the cuisine as 'Japanese' and the restaurant as 'Korean Cuisine - Funan Mall', but the dish name, preparation style, and Chinese name (铁板牛肉苏东套餐) are consistent with Singaporean zi char cuisine; cuisine has been corrected to 'Zi Char / Singapore Fusion'. 3. Halal status is marked false because beef sourcing and sauce ingredients (oyster sauce) cannot be confirmed as halal-certified from the available data. 4. The serving size of 350g is an estimate for a standard zi char set plate; actual restaurant portions may vary. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not directly measured for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for these food components.
